focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 7

The knock came before full light, three hard raps that did not wait for courtesy. Noreen had just shoved the last borrowed tin behind Peter's office files and gone to scrape frost from the inside of the pantry window with the back of a teaspoon. Elsie's two taps, pause, one at the scullery window still thudded in her wrists. She had the spoon in one hand and a damp tea towel in the other. The towel had frozen stiff where it hung over her shoulder. Peter was at the table behind her, penciling figures on the back of an old gas bill and adding up totals that looked cleaner than their life.

He did not look up. "Answer it."

He was still in shirtsleeves and waistcoat, spectacles low on his nose, the sort of neat that made her feel she'd smeared fingerprints on borrowed silver. He had shaved already. He always shaved before noon on weekdays even when the buses were off and half the town was walking on ice.

Noreen set the spoon down and flexed her fingers, the knuckles burning now that she had stopped moving. On the front step stood a young council clerk she had seen once at the clinic queue, and behind him a woman in a maroon coat with a clipboard tucked into the crook of her arm. The woman's lipstick had cracked into pale lines, dragged at the edges like paint laid down in a jolting lorry.

"Mrs Walsh?" the woman said. "Edith Jeffries, emergency office. We've an accommodation order for this address."

The clerk held out the paper like it was hot. A red stamp from Calderbank Borough Council covered the top half under the heading Emergency Billeting and Utility Support. The ink had run where melted snow had touched it and dried again.

"No," Peter said from the hallway behind Noreen, close enough that she felt his breath move her hair. "There is a mistake. My wi...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ore the paper in two, tucked one half under the clock on the mantel, and folded the other into her bra.

By noon the contractions had faded to occasional cramps, and the bleeding was only a rust mark on folded cloth. Keane could not get through by phone, but a note came by boy from the exchange saying she would visit as soon as weather allowed and that Noreen was to stay in bed, no lifting, no stairs unless absolutely necessary.

Peter read the note twice, folded it into a rectangle, and tucked it into his pocketbook beside wage slips.

"There," he said. "Official instruction. Rest for a day and this will pass."

Noreen held out her hand. "Give me that."

He stared at her, then gave her the note.

She smoothed it flat and pinned it beside the phone with the bread knife, right over the wallpaper seam where anyone could see it. Under it she pinned her own penciled list.

Elsie came back before dinner with chalk in her coat pocket and a jar of broth wrapped in newspaper. Noreen opened the dresser drawer, took out the spare front-door key, and pressed it into Elsie's palm.

"If this starts again and he says wait, you don't wait," Noreen said. "You come in and ring Keane."

Peter stiffened. "You are not distributing keys from this house."

"I just did," Noreen said.

When Elsie left again, Peter sat at the table with his jaw tight and his hands flat on the wood, not writing anything.

Noreen listened to the quiet and felt no comfort in that either.

The baby shifted once, slow as a fish turning under dark water. Noreen placed her hand there and counted, not contractions this time, but distances: from chair to phone, from phone to door, from door to Elsie's wall, and from that wall to first light, when Elsie would already be at her table doing sums before the lane fully woke.
